1: μὴ ἀπώσατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ — The aorist middle apōsato (push away, reject) anticipates the expected conclusion from Romans 10's hard language. Paul's immediate counter-evidence is autobiographical: καὶ γὰρ ἐγὼ Ἰσραηλίτης εἰμί — "For I too am an Israelite." The kai is emphatic: I myself am exhibit A of an un-rejected Israelite. The three-part pedigree — seed of Abraham, tribe of Benjamin — mirrors the pattern of Philippians 3:5, where the same credentials appear as grounds for confidence "in the flesh."
2: οὐκ ἀπώσατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ ὃν προέγνω — The relative clause hon proegnō (whom he foreknew) is the crux: election precedes any historical contingency, so rejection is structurally impossible. The verb proginōskō (G4267) in Paul never means mere foreknowledge in the informational sense; it means prior choice establishing relationship. Psalms 94:14 ("the Lord will not forsake his people") provides the ground; the Elijah episode provides the illustration.
3: Κύριε, τοὺς προφήτας σου ἀπέκτειναν — The quotation follows 1 Kings 19:10/14 (LXX). Elijah's complaint is the voice of prophetic despair: altars torn down, prophets killed, only I left (κἀγὼ ὑπελείφθην μόνος). Paul uses this as a type of the present crisis — the faithful remnant feels isolated, yet the divine answer will reveal otherwise.
4: κατέλιπον ἐμαυτῷ ἑπτακισχιλίους ἄνδρας — The divine response uses the perfect katelipon (I have kept/reserved for myself): the remnant is not self-selected but divinely preserved. The dative emautō — for myself — establishes the remnant as God's possession, not simply survivors. The phrase "who have not bowed the knee to Baal" is a concrete behavioral test; the negative formulation (a knee not bent) serves as the criterion.
5: λεῖμμα κατ᾽ ἐκλογὴν χάριτος γέγονεν — Leimma (G3005, remnant) picks up the Elijah language. Kat᾽ eklogēn charitos — according to election of grace: the genitive charitos qualifies the election. The perfect gegonen (has come to be and stands) marks the present reality of the remnant as established fact, not aspiration.
6: εἰ δὲ χάριτι, οὐκέτι ἐξ ἔργων — A logical disjunction Paul has already established in Romans 4 with Abraham: grace and works are mutually exclusive bases for the same standing. Epei hē charis ouketi ginetai charis — otherwise grace is no longer grace. The epei (since/otherwise) clause states the consequence of mixing the two: the category dissolves. Paul's argument is analytic, not moral: by definition, grace-based election cannot be simultaneously works-based.
7: τοῦτο οὗ ἐπιζητεῖ Ἰσραήλ, τοῦτο οὐκ ἐπέτυχεν — Epizēteō (G1934, seek earnestly) implies persistent pursuit. Epitykhein (to hit upon, attain, G2013) is the athletic-competition word for reaching the goal. Israel pursued but did not obtain; the elect obtained. οἱ δὲ λοιποὶ ἐπωρώθησαν — the rest were hardened: the divine passive of pōroō (G4456) — to calcify, to form a callous. The cognate noun pōrōsis (Romans 11:25) denotes the resulting condition.
8: ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ὁ θεὸς πνεῦμα κατανύξεως — The citation blends Deuteronomy 29:4 and Isaiah 29:10 (LXX). Katanyksis (G2659) is a hapax in the NT: stupor, numbness, a word for the deep insensibility induced by divine judgment. In Isaiah 29:10 the parallel is "the spirit of deep sleep." Paul attributes the stupor to God's giving (edōken, aorist), not to Israel's self-infliction — consistent with the divine-passive hardening of Romans 11:7.
9: Δαυὶδ λέγει· γενηθήτω ἡ τράπεζα αὐτῶν εἰς παγίδα — Psalms 69:22–23 (LXX Romans 68:22–23). The table (trapeza) that should provide sustenance becomes a snare (pagis) and a trap (thēra). Paul's use of David is deliberate: the Psalm is a suffering-righteous Psalm widely applied to Jesus in early Christianity (Psalms 69:9 at John 2:17; 69:21 at Matthew 27:34/John 19:29). The application to Israel's hardening draws from the same Psalm that elsewhere illuminates Christ's own rejection.
10: σκοτισθήτωσαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτῶν τοῦ μὴ βλέπειν — The continuation of Psalms 69:23: darkened eyes and permanently bent backs. The articular infinitive tou mē blepein expresses purpose or result — eyes darkened so as not to see. Dia pantos (continually, always) indicates the sustained character of the judgment, not a temporary condition. The image of the bent back under load closes the citation with a picture of servitude.
11: μὴ ἔπταισαν ἵνα πέσωσιν — The distinction is between ptaiō (G4417, to stumble) and piptō (G4098, to fall definitively). Did they stumble so as to fall irrecoverably? Mē genoito — by no means. The word paraptōmati (transgression, G3900) appears: their stumbling was a paraptoōma that occasioned Gentile inclusion. Parazēlōsai (G3863, to provoke to jealousy) picks up the motif from Deuteronomy 32:21 (cited at Romans 10:19): Israel's jealousy of Gentile blessing is part of the restorative plan.
12: εἰ δὲ τὸ παράπτωμα αὐτῶν πλοῦτος κόσμου — Paul constructs a qal wachomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if their failure (παράπτωμα) produces world-wealth, how much more will their plērōma produce? The word plērōma (G4138, fullness) here means their completion — their full inclusion. The contrast is παράπτωμα/ἥττημα (failure/defeat) against plērōma. The conclusion of the argument will come at Romans 11:26.
13: ὑμῖν λέγω τοῖς ἔθνεσιν — Paul pivots to direct address to Gentile readers. He asserts his apostolic identity: ἐφ᾽ ὅσον μέν εἰμι ἐγὼ ἐθνῶν ἀπόστολος — inasmuch as I am apostle of the Gentiles. The present tense eimi is timeless. δοξάζω τὴν διακονίαν μου — I glorify my ministry: the verb doxazō does not mean boasting; it means to recognize and honor the worth of what has been entrusted.
14: εἴ πως παραζηλώσω μου τὴν σάρκα — The optative parazelōsō (might provoke to jealousy) expresses contingent hope, not certainty. Tēn sarka mou — my flesh, i.e., my kinsmen according to biological descent (cf. Romans 9:3). The chain of purpose: Paul's Gentile mission creates Gentile blessing → Gentile blessing provokes Jewish jealousy → jealousy leads some to salvation. Paul presents his Gentile apostolate as indirectly missionary to Israel.
15: εἰ γὰρ ἡ ἀποβολὴ αὐτῶν καταλλαγὴ κόσμου — Apobolē (G580, casting away, rejection) is a rare word. Katallagē (G2643, reconciliation) appeared at Romans 5:11; it denotes the restored relational status between God and the world. If their rejection produced reconciliation, what will their acceptance produce? The answer: ζωὴ ἐκ νεκρῶν — life from the dead. Whether this refers to eschatological resurrection or to a spiritual revival of unprecedented scale, Paul leaves the image to press the point: the scale is incomprehensible.
16: εἰ δὲ ἡ ἀπαρχὴ ἁγία, καὶ τὸ φύραμα — Aparkhē (G536, firstfruit) and phyrama (G5445, lump of dough) draw on Numbers 15:20–21 where the dough offering consecrates the whole batch. The argument is one of representative holiness: the part that is holy sanctifies the whole. Paul applies the image in two registers: firstfruit/lump (temporal — the patriarchs sanctify the nation) and root/branches (organic — the root determines the character of what it supports).
17: εἰ δέ τινες τῶν κλάδων ἐξεκλάσθησαν — Exeklasthēsan (were broken off, aorist passive) marks a completed act. The Gentile reader is addressed as a ἀγριέλαιος (G65, wild olive, hapax in NT) grafted in ἐν αὐτοῖς (among them, the remaining branches). The shared benefit is τῆς ῥίζης τῆς πιότητος τῆς ἐλαίας — the root of the fatness (richness) of the olive tree. Piotēs (G4096, fatness, richness) is agricultural: the root's nutrient supply feeds all grafted branches equally.
18: μὴ κατακαυχῶ τῶν κλάδων — Katakaukhaō (G2620, to boast against, to exult over) is the specific wrong. The corrective is logical: οὐ σὺ τὴν ῥίζαν βαστάζεις ἀλλ᾽ ἡ ῥίζα σέ — you do not carry the root; the root carries you. The Gentile believer's continuance depends on the root (the patriarchal covenant/promise), not vice versa. The present tense bastazeis/se marks an ongoing structural reality.
19: ἐρεῖς οὖν — Paul quotes the imagined Gentile interlocutor: "Branches were broken off so that I might be grafted in." The passive exeklasthēsan (were broken off) is used again; the purpose hina egō enkentristhō presumes the Gentile's grafting was the goal of Israel's breaking off. Paul will grant the premise in Romans 11:20 but challenge the inference.
20: καλῶς· τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ ἐξεκλάσθησαν — Kalōs (well, rightly) concedes the factual claim: yes, broken off by unbelief. σὺ δὲ τῇ πίστει ἕστηκας — the dative of means: you stand by faith. The perfect hestēkas (you have stood and stand) marks a stable condition. The warning follows: μὴ ὑψηλὰ φρόνει ἀλλὰ φοβοῦ — do not think high thoughts but fear. Hypsēla phronein (to think loftily) is the same root as hypselophrosynē condemned in Romans 12:16.
21: εἰ γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τῶν κατὰ φύσιν κλάδων οὐκ ἐφείσατο — The aorist epheisato (spared, G5339): God did not spare the natural branches — those that belonged to the tree by origin. The argument is from the greater to the lesser: if not the natural, then certainly not you. The phrase κατὰ φύσιν (according to nature) reappears from 11:17 and anticipates the παρὰ φύσιν (against nature) of Romans 11:24 — the surprise of the Gentile grafting.
22: ἴδε οὖν χρηστότητα καὶ ἀποτομίαν θεοῦ — Paul juxtaposes chrēstotēs (G5544, kindness/goodness) and apotomia (G663, severity/steepness; from apotemnō, to cut off sharply). The latter is a hapax in the NT; it denotes abruptness, the quality of an abrupt cut. The condition for continued Gentile standing: ἐὰν ἐπιμένῃς τῇ χρηστότητι — if you remain in the kindness. The conditional ean + present subjunctive marks genuine contingency: the standing is real but not unconditional in its continuation.
23: ἐκεῖνοι δέ, ἐὰν μὴ ἐπιμένωσιν τῇ ἀπιστίᾳ, ἐγκεντρισθήσονται — The condition for Israel's re-grafting is removal of unbelief (apistia). The future passive enkentristhēsontai (will be grafted in) is the divine act awaiting the condition. The ground: δυνατὸς γάρ ἐστιν ὁ θεὸς πάλιν ἐγκεντρίσαι αὐτούς — God is able again to graft them in. Dynatos (G1415, powerful/able) emphasizes divine capacity against any human calculation that re-grafting is impossible.
24: εἰ γὰρ σὺ ἐκ τῆς κατὰ φύσιν ἐξεκόπης ἀγριελαίου — Paul now explicitly names the Gentile situation as παρὰ φύσιν (against nature): the wild olive being grafted into the cultivated tree defies normal horticultural logic (the cultivated is grafted into rootstock, not the other direction). If the unnatural grafting happened, the natural re-grafting is a fortiori — πολλῷ μᾶλλον (how much more) — achievable. The argument closes the olive tree metaphor.
25:
οὐ θέλω ὑμᾶς ἀγνοεῖν, ἀδελφοί, τὸ μυστήριον τοῦτο — The disclosure formula (ou thelō hymas agnoein — I do not want you to be ignorant) marks a formal revelation. Mystērion (G3466) in Pauline usage is not a puzzle but something formerly hidden and now divinely disclosed (cf. Romans 16:25–26; Colossians 1:26–27). The content: partial hardening (πώρωσις ἀπὸ μέρους) has come upon Israel until (ἄχρι οὗ) the fullness of the Gentiles enters.

Pōrōsis (G4457, hardening) is the medical word for the formation of callous or calcified tissue — not blindness but insensibility to pressure. Its qualified character (apo merous, in part) is critical: not total and not permanent. Plērōma tōn ethnōn (fullness/completion of the Gentiles) has the sense of the full quota or number: the complete Gentile ingathering that precedes Israel's restoration.
26: καὶ οὕτως πᾶς Ἰσραὴλ σωθήσεται — The adverb houtōs (in this way, thus) is key: it connects the mode of salvation to the process just described, not simply asserting a final result. "All Israel" is debated — whether it means the totality of ethnic Israel at a future point, or Israel as the full covenant people including Gentiles, or the full remnant elect of Israel through history. The citation follows: Isaiah 59:20–21 (LXX), where the Deliverer comes ek Siōn (from Zion) to turn ungodliness from Jacob. Paul adapts the LXX: the LXX has "for Zion's sake" (heneken Siōn); Paul's "from Zion" follows the MT more closely.
27: καὶ αὕτη αὐτοῖς ἡ παρ᾽ ἐμοῦ διαθήκη — The covenant formula from Isaiah 59:21 blended with Isaiah 27:9: "this is my covenant with them, when I take away their sins." Diathēkē (G1242, covenant) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew bĕrît. The content is the removal of sins — the new covenant language of Jeremiah 31:33–34 standing behind this whole cluster. Paul presents Israel's future restoration as a covenantal, not merely political, act.
28: κατὰ μὲν τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ἐχθροὶ δι᾽ ὑμᾶς — The double kata structure draws the tension: according to the gospel they are enemies (the result of their rejection); according to election they are beloved (the result of the patriarchal covenant). Di᾽ hymas (for your sake) marks the Gentile benefit of their present enmity. Di᾽ tous pateras (because of the fathers): the patriarchal covenant is the unconditional ground of continued divine love — not current obedience or faith.
29: ἀμεταμέλητα γὰρ τὰ χαρίσματα καὶ ἡ κλῆσις τοῦ θεοῦ — Ametamelētos (G278, irrevocable, without afterthought) is a legal/contractual term: a grant or call that cannot be recalled by the giver. Its semantic force is not emotional (God will not regret) but volitional and covenantal (God will not reverse). Charismata (G5486, gifts) here refers to the covenant gifts of Romans 9:4–5 (adoption, glory, covenants, law, worship, promises, patriarchs); klēsis (G2821, calling) refers to Israel's election-summons.
30: ὥσπερ γὰρ ὑμεῖς ποτε ἠπειθήσατε τῷ θεῷ — The past disobedience of the Gentiles (ēpeithēsate, aorist: a completed act) is now receiving mercy through Israel's disobedience. The syntax of Romans 11:30–32 creates a set of interlocking hōsper/houtōs clauses (just as… so also) building toward the universal mercy statement of Romans 11:32. The pattern works by analogy of positions: Gentiles once disobedient → now mercy; Israel now disobedient → future mercy.
31: οὕτως καὶ οὗτοι νῦν ἠπείθησαν τῷ ὑμετέρῳ ἐλέει — The unusual phrase tō hymetero eleei (by your mercy) — Israel's disobedience occasions mercy to Gentiles, and that mercy to Gentiles in turn occasions mercy to Israel. The dative tō… eleei is instrumental or causal. The double nun (now) in several manuscripts is difficult: "that they too now may receive mercy now." The repetition may be textual variation or a Pauline emphasis on the urgency and present timing of the merciful plan.
32: συνέκλεισεν γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τοὺς πάντας εἰς ἀπείθειαν — Synekleisen (G4788, shut up together, confined) is a strong aorist: God has enclosed all in disobedience. The verb denotes imprisonment or enclosure — all are shut in to the same condition. The purpose: ἵνα τοὺς πάντας ἐλεήσῃ — so that he might show mercy to all. The double tous pantas (the all, all of them — with article) is emphatic. This does not assert universal salvation; it asserts universal need and universal availability of mercy, corresponding to the Gentile/Jew scope of Romans 1–3.
33: Ὦ βάθος πλούτου καὶ σοφίας καὶ γνώσεως θεοῦ — The doxology breaks from argumentative prose into exclamation. The two adjectives are stacked with verbal nouns: anexeraunēta (G419, unsearchable, not subject to investigation — the root is the same as digging/mining: the riches cannot be mined out) and anexichniastoi (G421, past tracing out — from tracking footprints: his ways leave no trackable path). The genitives sou in Romans 11:34–35 mark quotation; the nouns krima (G2917, judgment) and hodos (G3598, way/path) encompass both his judicial decisions and his operational methods.
34: τίς γὰρ ἔγνω νοῦν κυρίου — Isaiah 40:13 (LXX). The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Noux Kyriou — the mind of the Lord. Paul uses nous (G3563) here in its sense of the reasoning capacity, the deliberative faculty. The parallel question — who has been his counselor (symboulou, G4825)? — extends the point: God's plan is not arrived at by consultation. The same Isaiah text will appear at 1 Corinthians 2:16 with a different argument.
35: ἢ τίς προέδωκεν αὐτῷ, καὶ ἀνταποδοθήσεται αὐτῷ — Job 41:11 (LXX Romans 41:3) or Isaiah 40:14. The commercial metaphor: who gave first, so that God must repay? Proedōken (G4272, give beforehand): no prior gift to God creates a debt. The future antapodothēsetai (will be repaid in return) is conditional on a prior gift — which the question establishes is impossible. Verse 35 closes the rhetorical triad with the economic impossibility of placing God under obligation.
36: ὅτι ἐξ αὐτοῦ καὶ δι᾽ αὐτοῦ καὶ εἰς αὐτὸν τὰ πάντα — Three prepositional phrases deploy three distinct prepositions: ek (from/out of — source), dia (through — mediation), eis (into/toward — goal). All things have God as source, instrument, and telos. The Stoic formula ex autou kai di᾽ autou kai eis auton is adapted: Paul appropriates the cosmological structure but gives it a personal subject. The doxology autō hē doxa eis tous aiōnas closes the doctrinal argument of Romans 9–11.
